steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage ends with 'This suggests that ______' — so the correct answer must follow logically from the evidence given. The key facts: a handedness study needs at least 176 individuals for a representative result, but both Lockard's and Goodall's studies each used only 8.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — neither the study by Lockard nor the study by Goodall provides sufficient evidence to make a meaningful comparison about handedness in primates: Both studies fall equally short of the 176-individual minimum. This follows directly from the evidence. ✓
  • Choice B — the study by Lockard reliably represents handedness in captive primates, but the study by Goodall likely does not reliably represent handedness in wild primates: Both studies used only 8 individuals — there is no basis for treating one as reliable and the other as not. ✗
  • Choice C — Goodall likely underestimated the prevalence of right-handedness among the wild chimpanzees in the study: The passage gives no basis for a directional claim about underestimation. The problem is sample size, not the direction of any bias. ✗
  • Choice D — the study by Lockard reliably represents handedness in captive primates but not in wild primates: Lockard studied captive gorillas, not wild primates — and with only 8 individuals, the study cannot be called reliable for any population. ✗
That matches Choice A — neither the study by Lockard nor the study by Goodall provides sufficient evidence to make a meaningful comparison about handedness in primates.
